author: Athanasius of Alexandria
work: On the Incarnation of the Word (De Incarnatione)
note: The 'divine dilemma' — God's goodness cannot let His handiwork perish. NOTE: this file adds the OTHER sections of the treatise; the famous 'great exchange' at §54 ('For He was made man that we might be made God…') already lives in patristics.json as id 'athanasius-inc-54' — do not duplicate it. Verified verbatim against source_url ('God's goodness' confirmed; apologia-citations, web, 2026-07-11).
text: For it were not worthy of God's goodness that the things He had made should waste away, because of the deceit practised on men by the devil.

author: Athanasius of Alexandria
work: On the Incarnation of the Word (De Incarnatione)
note: Why repentance alone could not restore humanity — it cannot recall men from their nature nor satisfy God's just claim. Verified verbatim against source_url (apologia-citations, web, 2026-07-11).
text: But repentance would, firstly, fail to guard the just claim of God. For He would still be none the more true, if men did not remain in the grasp of death; nor, secondly, does repentance call men back from what is their nature—it merely stays them from acts of sin.

author: Athanasius of Alexandria
work: On the Incarnation of the Word (De Incarnatione)
note: The Word 'takes unto Himself a body, and that of no different sort from ours' — a real, consubstantial humanity, not an appearance. Verified verbatim against source_url (apologia-citations, web, 2026-07-11).
text: He takes unto Himself a body, and that of no different sort from ours.

author: Athanasius of Alexandria
work: On the Incarnation of the Word (De Incarnatione)
note: The Word restoring the image of God in man so that men might know Him again. Verified verbatim against source_url (apologia-citations, web, 2026-07-11).
text: What then was God to do? or what was to be done save the renewing of that which was in God's image, so that by it men might once more be able to know Him?

author: Athanasius of Alexandria
work: On the Incarnation of the Word (De Incarnatione)
note: The portrait analogy — the effaced likeness renewed only by the one whose likeness it is coming again in person. Robertson's NPNF wording (NOT the copyrighted CSMV/Popular Patristics 'You know how it is when some portrait…'). The full sentence begins 'For as, when the likeness…'; the quote below preserves the load-bearing clause. Verified verbatim against source_url (apologia-citations, web, 2026-07-11).
text: When the likeness painted on a panel has been effaced by stains from without, he whose likeness it is must needs come once more to enable the portrait to be renewed on the same wood.

author: Athanasius of Alexandria
work: On the Incarnation of the Word (De Incarnatione)
note: Why the death was public and not a private, natural death — a hidden death would have been mistaken for mere human weakness. Verified verbatim against source_url (apologia-citations, web, 2026-07-11).
text: If, then, He had laid aside His body somewhere in private, and upon a bed, after the manner of men, it would have been thought that He also did this agreeably to the weakness of His nature, and because there was nothing in him more than in other men.

author: Athanasius of Alexandria
work: On the Incarnation of the Word (De Incarnatione)
note: Why the death had to be on the cross specifically — to bear the curse ('Cursed is he that hangeth on a tree') and, with outstretched hands, to draw Jew and Gentile alike. The ellipsis marks the omitted span between the two sentences within §25. Verified verbatim against source_url (apologia-citations, web, 2026-07-11).
text: For if He came Himself to bear the curse laid upon us, how else could He have 'become a curse,' unless He received the death set for a curse? and that is the Cross. For this is exactly what is written: 'Cursed is he that hangeth on a tree.' … Whence it was fitting for the Lord to bear this also and to spread out His hands, that with the one He might draw the ancient people, and with the other those from the Gentiles, and unite both in Himself.

author: Athanasius of Alexandria
work: On the Incarnation of the Word (De Incarnatione)
note: The reality of the resurrection argued from the fact that death is now trampled underfoot by all who belong to Christ. Verified verbatim against source_url (apologia-citations, web, 2026-07-11).
text: For if, as our argument shewed, death has been brought to nought, and because of Christ all tread him under foot, much more did He Himself first tread him down with His own body, and bring him to nought.

author: Athanasius of Alexandria
work: On the Incarnation of the Word (De Incarnatione)
note: The living Christ known by His ongoing acts — a dead man works nothing, yet Christ still expels demons and overturns idolatry. Verified verbatim against source_url (apologia-citations, web, 2026-07-11).
text: For it is plain that if Christ be dead, He could not be expelling demons and spoiling idols; for a dead man the spirits would not have obeyed.

author: Athanasius of Alexandria
work: On the Incarnation of the Word (De Incarnatione)
note: Creation out of nothing and the special grace given to man alone — made after God's own image, given a portion of the Word so as to reflect Him and abide in blessedness. Sets up the whole treatise: what is lost in the Fall is this image/reflexion, which the Incarnation restores. Transcribed from the CCEL leaf page; verified verbatim (apologia-citations, web, 2026-07-13).
text: He gave them a further gift, and He did not barely create man, as He did all the irrational creatures on the earth, but made them after His own image, giving them a portion even of the power of His own Word; so that having as it were a kind of reflexion of the Word, and being made rational, they might be able to abide ever in blessedness, living the true life which belongs to the saints in paradise.

author: Athanasius of Alexandria
work: On the Incarnation of the Word (De Incarnatione)
note: The Fall — man was made for incorruption but, despising the contemplation of God, incurred the condemnation of death and no longer remained as he was made, corruption gaining mastery 'as king.' The parenthetical '(as was said in the former treatise)' is retained to keep the quote verbatim. Transcribed from the CCEL leaf page; verified verbatim (apologia-citations, web, 2026-07-13).
text: Thus, then, God has made man, and willed that he should abide in incorruption; but men, having despised and rejected the contemplation of God, and devised and contrived evil for themselves (as was said in the former treatise), received the condemnation of death with which they had been threatened; and from thenceforth no longer remained as they were made, but were being corrupted according to their devices; and death had the mastery over them as king.

author: Athanasius of Alexandria
work: On the Incarnation of the Word (De Incarnatione)
note: The Fall deepened — the life 'in correspondence with God' freely given by grace was forfeited when men, by counsel of the devil, turned to corruption and became the cause of their own death. Transcribed from the CCEL leaf page; verified verbatim (apologia-citations, web, 2026-07-13).
text: For God has not only made us out of nothing; but He gave us freely, by the Grace of the Word, a life in correspondence with God. But men, having rejected things eternal, and, by counsel of the devil, turned to the things of corruption, became the cause of their own corruption in death.

author: Athanasius of Alexandria
work: On the Incarnation of the Word (De Incarnatione)
note: The prophecy argument against 'the disbelief of the Jews' — Scripture foretold both the virgin birth (Isa. 7:14, 'Lo, the Virgin shall be with child…Emmanuel') and the coming Messiah (Num. 24:17, 'There shall rise a star out of Jacob'). Athanasius argues the Jews may be answered from the very Scriptures they themselves read. Transcribed from the CCEL leaf page; verified verbatim (apologia-citations, web, 2026-07-13).
text: For prophets proclaimed beforehand concerning the wonder of the Virgin and the birth from her, saying: "Lo, the Virgin shall be with child, and shall bring forth a Son, and they shall call his name Emmanuel, which is, being interpreted, God with us." But Moses, the truly great, and whom they believe to speak truth, with reference to the Saviour's becoming man, having estimated what was said as important, and assured of its truth, set it down in these words: "There shall rise a star out of Jacob, and a man out of Israel, and he shall break in pieces the captains of Moab."

author: Athanasius of Alexandria
work: On the Incarnation of the Word (De Incarnatione)
note: Refutation of the Gentiles / the church as living evidence — the oracles that once filled every place have fallen silent since the coming of the Word, the old gods (Zeus, Cronos, Apollo) are exposed as mortal men, and Christ alone is recognised as the true God. The ellipsis marks omitted text between the two sentences within §47. Transcribed from the CCEL leaf page; verified verbatim (apologia-citations, web, 2026-07-13).
text: And whereas formerly every place was full of the deceit of the oracles, and demons used to deceive men's fancy, occupying springs or rivers, trees or stones, and thus imposed upon the simple by their juggleries; now, after the divine visitation of the Word, their deception has ceased. … now that the Saviour has appeared among men, those others have been exposed as mortal men, and Christ alone has been recognised among men as the true God, the Word of God.

author: Athanasius of Alexandria
work: On the Incarnation of the Word (De Incarnatione)
note: The fruit of the resurrection in changed lives — no philosopher or king ever remade a multitude as Christ does, so that 'whoremongers are chaste, and murderers no longer hold the sword,' and only the Cross and Resurrection give real assurance of immortality. The ellipsis marks omitted text within §50. Transcribed from the CCEL leaf page; verified verbatim (apologia-citations, web, 2026-07-13).
text: Or who has so rid men of the passions of the natural man, that whoremongers are chaste, and murderers no longer hold the sword, and those who were formerly mastered by cowardice play the man? … Or who else has given men such assurance of immortality, as has the Cross of Christ, and the Resurrection of His Body?

author: Athanasius of Alexandria
work: On the Incarnation of the Word (De Incarnatione)
note: The conclusion — the Scriptures are truly known only through a pure life; 'he that would comprehend the mind of those who speak of God must needs begin by washing and cleansing his soul.' Ellipses mark omitted text within §57. Transcribed from the CCEL leaf page; verified verbatim (apologia-citations, web, 2026-07-13).
text: But for the searching of the Scriptures and true knowledge of them, an honourable life is needed, and a pure soul, and that virtue which is according to Christ… For without a pure mind and a modelling of the life after the saints, a man could not possibly comprehend the words of the saints. … thus he that would comprehend the mind of those who speak of God must needs begin by washing and cleansing his soul, by his manner of living, and approach the saints themselves by imitating their works.
